meta.title: PHP
meta.description: Review checklist for PHP applications (Laravel, Symfony, WordPress)

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the change, the problem it solves, and any relevant context.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers without context waste time reverse-engineering intent from code. A clear description speeds up review and creates a searchable historical record.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the code?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add more context to the PR description? It would help to know why we are restructuring the service layer.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves more reviewer time than it costs to write.

items.text: Is the change scoped appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each PR should do one thing. Mixing a bug fix with a refactor makes review harder and rollbacks riskier.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large multi-concern PRs are slow to review and difficult to revert if one part causes issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR touches multiple unrelated areas. Could any part ship independently?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds the new API endpoint and also refactors the middleware stack. Could we split these into two PRs?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per PR. If you need a paragraph to explain what it does, it is probably too big.

items.text: Is dead code removed rather than commented out?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code that is no longer used should be deleted, not commented out. Version control preserves history.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Commented-out code creates confusion about whether it is temporarily disabled or leftover. It adds noise and often never gets cleaned up.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for blocks of commented-out code or unused functions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This commented-out controller method has been here since the migration. Since we have git history, let us remove it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Delete dead code. Git remembers.

items.text: Do classes, methods, and variables follow PSR naming conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PHP-FIG PSR standards define camelCase for methods/variables, PascalCase for classes, and UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent naming reduces cognitive load when reading unfamiliar code and helps IDE autocompletion work predictably.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that class names are PascalCase, methods are camelCase, and constants are UPPER_SNAKE_CASE. Verify namespace casing matches directory structure.
baseContent.exampleComment: This method uses snake_case (get_user_data) but PSR standards prefer camelCase (getUserData).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow PSR naming conventions for consistency across the PHP ecosystem.

items.text: Are parameter and return type declarations used?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PHP 7+ supports scalar type declarations, return types, nullable types, and union types. PHP 8+ adds intersection types and enums.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Type declarations catch bugs at runtime (or statically with PHPStan/Psalm), serve as documentation, and enable IDE autocompletion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new or modified function signatures for missing type hints. Look for mixed or no return type.
baseContent.exampleComment: This method accepts and returns untyped values. Adding type declarations would help catch misuse early.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Type every parameter and return value. Use strict_types where possible.

items.text: Is declare(strict_types=1) used in new files?

baseContent.whatItMeans: strict_types enforces scalar type declarations at call sites, preventing silent type coercion like '5' being accepted as int.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without strict_types, PHP silently coerces types which can mask bugs. A string '0' coercing to int 0 has caused real production issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the first line of new PHP files for declare(strict_types=1).
baseContent.exampleComment: This new file is missing declare(strict_types=1). Adding it prevents silent type coercion bugs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always declare strict_types=1 at the top of new PHP files.

items.text: Are exceptions caught at appropriate boundaries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Exceptions should be caught at service boundaries, not swallowed silently or caught too broadly with catch (Exception $e).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Catching too broadly hides bugs. Not catching at boundaries leaks implementation details to callers or crashes the application.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for catch (Exception $e) or catch (Throwable $e) blocks. Check if they log/rethrow or silently swallow.
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch block swallows all exceptions silently. At minimum we should log the error.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Catch specific exceptions. Log or rethrow. Never silently swallow errors.

items.text: Is null handled explicitly rather than relying on falsy checks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PHP treats null, 0, '', false, and [] all as falsy. Using if ($value) when you mean if ($value !== null) can cause subtle bugs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A string value of '0' or integer 0 is falsy in PHP, which can silently skip valid data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for if ($var) checks where null-specific checks (=== null or !== null) would be more correct.
baseContent.exampleComment: This checks if ($username) but an empty string could be a valid edge case. Should this be if ($username !== null)?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use strict null checks (=== null) instead of relying on PHP's loose truthiness.

items.text: Are database queries parameterized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: User input must never be concatenated directly into SQL queries. Use prepared statements or the query builder's parameter binding.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection is one of the most common and devastating web vulnerabilities. It can lead to data theft, modification, or complete database compromise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for string concatenation in SQL queries. Look for DB::raw() with unescaped variables. Verify whereRaw() uses bindings.
baseContent.exampleComment: This query concatenates user input directly into SQL. Use parameter binding to prevent SQL injection.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use parameterized queries. Never concatenate user input into SQL.

items.text: Is output properly escaped to prevent XSS?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Any user-provided content rendered in HTML must be escaped. Blade's {{ }} auto-escapes, but {!! !!} does not.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Cross-site scripting allows attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript in other users' browsers, stealing sessions or credentials.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for {!! !!} in Blade templates and echo statements without htmlspecialchars(). Verify any raw output is intentional and sanitized.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses {!! $userComment !!} which renders raw HTML. Unless this is intentionally sanitized, use {{ $userComment }} instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use {{ }} in Blade, htmlspecialchars() in raw PHP. Only use raw output for trusted, sanitized content.

items.text: Are mass assignment protections in place?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In Laravel, models should define $fillable or $guarded to prevent users from setting arbitrary columns via request data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without mass assignment protection, an attacker could set is_admin=true or other sensitive fields by adding extra form fields.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new or modified Eloquent models for $fillable or $guarded. Look for Model::create($request->all()) without validation.
baseContent.exampleComment: This model has no $fillable defined and uses $request->all() to create records. An attacker could set any column.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always define $fillable on models and validate input before mass assignment.

items.text: Are forms and state-changing routes protected against CSRF?

baseContent.whatItMeans: POST/PUT/DELETE routes should verify a CSRF token. Laravel includes this via the @csrf Blade directive and VerifyCsrfToken middleware.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without CSRF protection, an attacker can trick authenticated users into submitting malicious requests from another site.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that forms include @csrf and that routes are not excluded from CSRF middleware without good reason.
baseContent.exampleComment: This form is missing @csrf. Without it, the route is vulnerable to cross-site request forgery.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Include @csrf in all forms. Only exclude routes from CSRF middleware for webhook endpoints with their own verification.

items.text: Are secrets and credentials kept out of source code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API keys, database passwords, and encryption keys should come from environment variables or a secret manager, not hardcoded in PHP files.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded secrets in source code can be leaked through version control, logs, or error pages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for hardcoded strings that look like passwords, API keys, or tokens. Check that .env is gitignored.
baseContent.exampleComment: This API key is hardcoded in the service class. Move it to .env and reference it via config().
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use environment variables for all secrets. Never commit .env files.

items.text: Is dependency injection used instead of static calls or facades where testability matters?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Classes should receive dependencies through constructor injection rather than using static methods, globals, or untestable facade calls.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, enables unit testing with mocks, and prevents hidden coupling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for new ClassName() inside methods, static method calls to service classes, or direct use of global state.
baseContent.exampleComment: This controller instantiates the PaymentService directly. Injecting it via the constructor would make this testable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Inject dependencies through the constructor. It makes code testable and dependencies visible.

items.text: Is business logic kept out of controllers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Controllers should delegate to services, actions, or domain objects rather than containing complex business logic directly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fat controllers are hard to test, hard to reuse, and tend to grow uncontrollably as features are added.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if controller methods exceed 15-20 lines or contain complex conditionals, loops, or multiple database calls.
baseContent.exampleComment: This controller method has 60 lines of business logic. Consider extracting it into a service or action class.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Controllers should validate, delegate, and respond. Business logic belongs in services.

items.text: Are N+1 query problems avoided with eager loading?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Accessing a relationship in a loop without eager loading triggers one query per iteration instead of a single joined query.
baseContent.whyItMatters: N+1 queries can cause hundreds of database queries for a single page load, severely degrading performance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for relationship access inside loops (foreach ($posts as $post) { $post->author->name }). Check for missing with() or load() calls.
baseContent.exampleComment: This loop accesses $order->customer inside a foreach without eager loading. Use Order::with('customer')->get() to avoid N+1 queries.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always eager-load relationships you know you will access in a loop.

items.text: Is request input validated before use?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All user input should be validated using form requests, validation rules, or explicit checks before being used in business logic.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated input can cause unexpected types, missing fields, or malicious data to reach business logic or the database.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that controller methods validate input before using it. Look for $request->input() used without prior validation.
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint uses $request->email directly without validation. Use a FormRequest or $request->validate().
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate all input at the boundary. Use FormRequest classes for complex validation.

items.text: Are tests included for new or changed behavior?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features and bug fixes should include tests that verify the behavior. PHPUnit for unit tests, feature tests for HTTP endpoints.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without tests, regressions go undetected until they hit production. Tests document expected behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new test files or additions to existing test files. Verify edge cases and error paths are covered.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new service method has no tests. Could we add a unit test covering the happy path and the error case?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every behavioral change should have a corresponding test.

items.text: Are database migrations reversible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations should implement a down() method that reverses the up() changes, enabling rollbacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If a deployment fails, irreversible migrations prevent quick rollbacks, turning a minor issue into a major incident.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that migration files have a down() method that correctly reverses the schema changes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a column but the down() method is empty. Add Schema::dropColumn() for rollback support.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always implement down() in migrations. Test rollbacks before deploying.

items.text: Will this change work across all environments (dev, staging, production)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changes should not depend on environment-specific state like local file paths, specific PHP extensions, or dev-only services.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Code that works locally but fails in production causes incidents and erodes confidence in deployments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded paths, environment-specific assumptions, or dependencies not listed in composer.json.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses file_get_contents with an absolute path. Use storage_path() or a config value for portability.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write code that works the same everywhere. Use config and environment variables for differences.
